experiences.intentions: The board is empty except for the active T piece spawning near the top in its flat orientation. Candidate moves like left/right or rotation can fine-tune future placement, but with no existing structure to fit around, immediately dropping is strongest and preserves simplicity. Hard dropping the T places it quickly onto the floor in its current useful orientation, giving a flat-ish early foundation.

experiences.intentions: Active piece is a Z near the top in vertical orientation. The stack below is low-to-mid with some unevenness, but there is no immediate threat at the top. Goal is to place this Z to improve future fit with upcoming O,T,J,L and avoid creating overhangs. From current spawn, simply hard dropping the vertical Z would place it on the right side over existing columns around the S/Z structure, likely creating a reasonable surface without wasting time; lateral movement or rotation is possible later, but with one action now the strongest progress toward placement is to commit downward. Hard drop advances the sub-goal of safely locking the piece while keeping the board manageable.

Since O rotation has no effect, rotate actions are useless. The stack below is somewhat uneven, but directly under the current O position there is a deep open center column area; the fastest productive move is to commit the O downward to start filling central space cleanly. Left/right could refine placement later, but with only one action now, hard_drop makes immediate progress and safely places the non-rotating piece.

experiences.intentions: The active T is high near the center with plenty of lateral freedom. The stack below is uneven but the most useful immediate opportunity is around the center-right where a T can be oriented to set up/fit future surfaces without committing to a bad hard drop. Goal now is to improve orientation before descent. Among actions, rotate_right changes the T from flat-top to a vertical-right shape that is more flexible for sliding into central notches on subsequent moves; moving left/right first is less committal but misses the key shape change, and dropping now would be premature from this height.

experiences.intentions: The active T is high above the stack with no immediate danger. The surface below has a notable central-right cavity around columns 4-6 in the lower-middle, and the next queue J,L,J,S suggests preserving flexibility rather than locking the T flat immediately. Current orientation is vertical-up (rotation 1). Rotating left moves to rotation 0, which gives a flat T-top shape that is generally easier to slide under overhangs and can prepare a T placement into central gaps while still high enough to maneuver next turn. Left/right movement now is less committal, but changing to the more versatile horizontal T orientation is the best sub-goal.

experiences.intentions: The active J piece is high and currently in a safe spawn area with no immediate top-out threat. The board surface is uneven with a notable central-right cavity structure; preserving flexibility is more important than instantly dropping the J in its current orientation. A useful sub-goal is to move the J toward the right side where its hook can potentially fit future ledges without blocking the flatter left-center lanes needed for upcoming L/J/S/I pieces. Among actions, right advances that plan immediately while keeping all rotation/drop options open; hard_drop/soft_drop commit too early, no_op wastes tempo, and rotating now is premature before lateral alignment.
experiences.tasks: Clear as many lines as possible in Tetris by placing tetrominoes strategically.

experiences.intentions: Current active piece is J near the upper-right, with a fairly uneven stack and no immediate line clear available from its present height. The main goal now is to place the J cleanly to avoid creating overhangs while setting up future L/J/S/I placements. At the current spawn area, moving laterally first is safer than dropping immediately; rotating now creates a more awkward vertical hook in open air, while hard/soft drop would commit to the right side without alignment. Shifting left gives more flexibility to target central cavities on subsequent moves and preserves options for later rotation/drop.
